title: ANH00029 Prevention of Deep Vein Thrombosis (DVT) and Pulmonary Embolism
text_en: If you experience symptoms of PE, please tell your doctor or nurse immediately. These symptoms may include shortness of breath, a sudden sharp pain in the chest, a feeling of lightheadedness or dizziness, rapid or irregular heartbeat, or coughing that produces

file: ANH00029_Prevention of Deep Vein Thrombosis (DVT) and Pulmonary Embolism.mp4
title: ANH00029 Prevention of Deep Vein Thrombosis (DVT) and Pulmonary Embolism
text_en: To help prevent deep vein thrombosis and pulmonary embolism, your doctor may require you to do the following. Walk around periodically to keep the blood from pooling in your legs, wear compression stockings, and take anti-clothing medication.

title: ANH11047 Atrial Fibrillation
text_en: Atrial fibrillation is a type of arrhythmia, which is an abnormality in the pace or force of your heartbeat. If you have atrial fibrillation, the heart's upper chambers contract in a fast, twitchy fashion, resulting in an irregular, uncoordinated heartbeat.

title: ANH11047 Atrial Fibrillation
text_en: Your heart is a muscular organ, made up of four chambers, the right and left atria, and the right and left ventricles. During a normal heartbeat, the chambers of your heart contract and relax in a rhythmic pattern to deliver a consistent flow of blood throughout your body.

title: ANH11047 Atrial Fibrillation
text_en: The right atrium collects blood from the body, and the right ventricle pumps it to the lungs, which bring oxygen to and remove carbon dioxide from the blood.

title: ANH11047 Atrial Fibrillation
text_en: At the same time, the left atrium receives blood from the lungs, and the left ventricle pumps it out to supply your body's tissues with oxygen and nutrients.

title: ANH11047 Atrial Fibrillation
text_en: Each heartbeat begins in the heart's natural pacemaker called the Sinoatrial or SA node located in the wall of the right atrium. An electrical signal starts here, then spreads across both atria, causing them to contract and push blood into the ventricles.

title: ANH11047 Atrial Fibrillation
text_en: As the atria relax, the signal travels through the atrial ventricular or AV node to the ventricular walls, causing them to contract. The ventricles relax, completing one full heartbeat. Immediately the SA node generates a new electrical impulse, repeating the pattern.

title: ANH11047 Atrial Fibrillation
text_en: In atrial fibrillation, the electrical signals originate from unusual locations in the atria and nearby pulmonary veins. These abnormal signals travel through the atria haphazardly, causing the atria to contract in a jumble of rapid quivers and twitches.

title: ANH11047 Atrial Fibrillation
text_en: The signals bombard the AV node, causing the ventricles to contract rapidly. Even at a faster pace, the ventricular contractions cannot keep up with the rapid atrial contractions, resulting in an uncordenated heartbeat.

title: ANH11047 Atrial Fibrillation
text_en: After these irregular contractions, the chambers may not empty completely, when blood collects in the atria, clots may form. A clot can flow out of your heart, through your neck, into your brain, where it can cause

title: ANH11047 Atrial Fibrillation
text_en: If your blood, pooling in your ventricles, deprives your body's tissues of oxygen and nutrients.

title: ANH11047 Atrial Fibrillation
text_en: If your heart is consistently unable to pump enough blood to meet the body's needs, you can develop heart failure.

title: ANH11047 Atrial Fibrillation
text_en: Your doctor may treat your atrial fibrillation, with anti-arhythmic medications. Your atrial fibrillation control the electrical signals sent to your atria, which help your heart beat in a regular rhythm.

title: ANH11047 Atrial Fibrillation
text_en: After treatment, your heart may still beat too quickly. In this case, your doctor may give you beta-blocker medications, to slow your heart rate down to

title: ANH11047 Atrial Fibrillation
text_en: In addition, your doctor may recommend procedures that restore your heart's normal rhythm and rate, such as cardioversion, ablation, or pacemaker placement.

title: ANH11047 Atrial Fibrillation
text_en: In electrical cardioversion, your doctor will give your heart a shock, to start a normal heart rhythm.

title: ANH11047 Atrial Fibrillation
text_en: During ablation, your surgeon will use radio wave energy to destroy the heart tissue generating the abnormal electrical signals.

title: ANH11047 Atrial Fibrillation
text_en: In pacemaker placement, your surgeon will implant a small device under the skin near your heart to regulate your heart's rhythm.

title: ANH11056 Folic Acid
text_en: If you are pregnant, you can take folic acid to help prevent certain birth defects in Folic acid and folate are two forms of the same B vitamin. Folate is the form that occurs naturally in foods, while folic acid is the man-made form added to multivitamins and fortified foods.

title: ANH11056 Folic Acid
text_en: Your body needs folic acid to help make new cells, including brain cells, blood cells, and DNA and RNA, the genetic material inside your cells.

title: ANH11056 Folic Acid
text_en: During pregnancy, you need even more folic acid because your baby's body needs it too. Folic acid helps your baby's brain and spinal cord develop normally. Taking folic acid can significantly reduce the risk of neural tube defects in your developing baby, called an embryo at this stage.

title: ANH11056 Folic Acid
text_en: The neural tube begins as a tiny oval-shaped piece of embryonic tissue, which folds into a tube about 28 days after conception. When this tube closes properly, it continues to develop into your baby's brain and spinal

title: ANH11056 Folic Acid
text_en: If the neural tube does not close due to the lack of folic acid in your diet, your baby may have a neural tube defect, such as spina bifida or anencephaly.

title: ANH11056 Folic Acid
text_en: In spina bifida, the neural tube fails to close at the bottom. As a result, the parts of the spine that enclose and protect the lower spinal cord do not completely form, allowing the cord to protrude. Children with this defect often require surgery and have permanent disabilities, including leg paralysis, developmental delays, and nervous system problems.

title: ANH11056 Folic Acid
text_en: In the condition called anencephaly, the neural tube fails to close at the top, preventing large parts of the brain and skull from developing completely. These with this defect are unable to live and usually die before birth or soon after.

title: ANH11056 Folic Acid
text_en: Folic acid may also protect against other birth defects, such as cleft lip and palate, and atrial septal defect, which is when a hole between the right and left upper chambers of the heart, fails to close during development.

title: ANH11056 Folic Acid
text_en: As the neural tube develops so early in pregnancy, often before a woman knows she's pregnant, experts recommend all women who are able to become pregnant take a daily multivitamin containing 400 micrograms of folic acid every day. During pregnancy, the need increases to 600 micrograms of folic acid each day.

title: ANH11056 Folic Acid
text_en: Experts recommend you take this multivitamin in addition to the food folate in your diet, as your body does not absorb folate found naturally in food, as easily as the man made folic acid. Caregivers will often prescribe even 1,000 micrograms of folic acid daily to assure these minimal levels are covered. Even more may be required in some groups of women, including those with previous miscarriages and those women deficient in a folic acid metabolic enzyme. Talk with your caregiver about the optimal dose for you.

title: ANH12061 Aortic Aneurysm and Dissection
text_en: The aorta is the largest artery in your body. Your heart pumps fresh, oxygen-rich blood through your aorta to deliver oxygen and nutrients to all your organs and tissues.
